title: Funding for innovations key for India to remain vaccine leader: Cyrus Poonawalla
summary: Speaking to ET after receiving the Lifetime Achievement Award for 2025 as part of the ET Awards for Corporate Excellence, Cyrus Poonawalla, founder and managing director, Serum Institute of India, stressed on the need for greater investments in R&D,...

contentHtml:
Synopsis

Speaking to ET after receiving the Lifetime Achievement Award for 2025 as part of the ET Awards for Corporate Excellence, Cyrus Poonawalla, founder and managing director, Serum Institute of India, stressed on the need for greater investments in R&D, accelerated regulatory approvals and making supply chains stronger to help retain India's global leadership position in vaccines.

Listen to this article in summarized format

India has the capability to deliver world-class, affordable vaccines, thanks to a robust talent pool, manufacturing scale, and a growing ecosystem, said Cyrus Poonawalla, founder and managing director, Serum Institute of India.

Speaking to ET after receiving the Lifetime Achievement Award for 2025 as part of the ET Awards for Corporate Excellence, Poonawalla, however, stressed on the need for greater investments in R&D, accelerated regulatory approvals and making supply chains stronger to help retain India's global leadership position in vaccines. Edited excerpts:

How does it feel to receive ET's Lifetime Achievement Award?

It is always humbling to be recognised, especially by a platform like The Economic Times that has chronicled India's growth story so closely. I see this not as a personal accolade, but as a recognition of the work done by thousands of people at Serum Institute over the past several decades. It is a reminder that our efforts in making vaccines affordable and accessible have had a meaningful impact, and that responsibility continues.

As the founder of SII, what does this award mean for your organisation?It is an affirmation of the path we chose very early on...to focus on scale, affordability, and quality. We operated in a space that was once dominated by multinational companies, and we demonstrated that it is possible to deliver world-class vaccines from India to the world. This recognition belongs equally to our scientists, our partners, and our collaborators globally who have trusted us over the years.

Also Read: Iran war impact: Essential drugs may cost up to 5% more, for now

How do you describe your six-decade-long journey of building Serum Institute?

It has been a journey driven more by purpose than design. When we started, the objective was simple-to ensure that life-saving vaccines shouldn't be out of reach for people because of cost or supply constraints. Over time, we built capabilities, invested in technology, and expanded globally, but the core principles remained unchanged. There have been challenges at every stage, but consistency of vision and long-term thinking have helped us build what Serum Institute is today.

To the next generation of vaccine scientists, what is your advice?

I would say focus on impact at scale. Scientific excellence is essential, but equally important is understanding where the need is greatest. The future of vaccines will depend not just on innovation, but on how effectively those innovations can be delivered to populations at scale. Remain patient, be rigorous in your work, and always keep the larger public good in mind.

Also Read: Drug regulator halves approval timelines, eases several rules

What are the key factors that can strengthen India's vaccine capabilities?India already has strong fundamentals, scientific talent, manufacturing scale, and a growing ecosystem. We need deeper investment in R&D, stronger collaboration between academia, industry, and government, and faster regulatory pathways without compromising on safety. Building long-term funding mechanisms for innovation and strengthening supply chains will also be important if India is to remain a global leader in vaccines.

Where do you see SII in the next 2-3 decades?I see Serum Institute continuing to evolve as a global vaccine innovator. The focus will be on new platforms, emerging diseases, and preparedness for future pandemics. Affordability will remain central to our mission. With the next generation now leading operations, I am confident that the organisation will continue to grow while staying true to its founding values.

What are the few things that you think could have been done better at your organisation?In hindsight, there are always areas where one could have moved faster, particularly expanding global partnerships sooner. However, we have generally taken a cautious and sustainable approach, which has helped us build long-term credibility. If anything, we will continue to place greater emphasis on accelerating innovation while maintaining the quality and reliability that we are known for.
